prior.full_brief:
# GreenBiz Supply Chain — Sustainability Intelligence Brief

## Market Signal Brief: Supply Chain Decarbonization Intelligence Landscape

---

# Purpose and Context of This Document

This brief covers the supply chain sustainability intelligence landscape as published and tracked by Trellis Group (formerly GreenBiz), synthesised with corroborating sources on Scope 3 reporting obligations, AI-driven decarbonisation research, and the 2026 regulatory environment affecting supply chain emissions disclosure. The brief is anchored to the workspace's role as an international freight forwarder operating across air (primary), road (secondary), and ocean (tertiary) modes, with cargo verticals spanning high-value and time-critical freight.

**Convention notes:** All binding-verb statements in this brief are either verbatim-sourced FACTs with inline citations, labeled inferences, or routed to *Legal Confirmation Required:* callouts. No regulatory conclusions are asserted without grounding. Dates reflect the UTC generation timestamp for this brief.

---

# Section 1: What's Moving and What Triggered It

**MONITORING**

The supply chain sustainability intelligence space is undergoing a structural shift across three simultaneous axes, each of which creates direct operational or commercial pressure for freight-focused workspaces.

**Axis 1 — Editorial and industry agenda-setting.** Trellis Group (formerly GreenBiz) publishes a dedicated supply chain topic hub tracking corporate decarbonisation, circular economy, and emissions accounting across freight-relevant sectors. Current editorial coverage as of April–May 2026 includes: the Greenhouse Gas Protocol's proposed expansion of emissions accounting scope; AI's application to supply chain waste and emissions avoidance; and next-generation textile supply chain risk. *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, April 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/

Specifically, Trellis editorial notes: "Greenhouse Gas Protocol mulls dramatic expansion of emissions accounting — The protocol's proposal builds on new guidelines, released this week, for how companies can claim benefits for investments in supply-chain decarbonization." *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, April 14, 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/

*Analytical inference:* If adopted, a GHG Protocol scope expansion would directly affect how freight forwarders' clients account for the emissions embedded in logistics services — potentially increasing the granularity of data that cargo principals request from carriers and forwarders for Scope 3 reporting purposes.

**Axis 2 — Regulatory fragmentation on climate disclosure.** The US SEC Climate Disclosure Rule, finalized in March 2024, has effectively stalled at the federal level following the SEC's March 2025 vote to end its legal defense. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

In this regulatory vacuum, state-level mandates are advancing. California's SB 253 now carries a firm deadline: "large enterprises doing business in California must report their Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions by August 10, 2026. Scope 3 reporting is set to follow in 2027." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

At the international level, "the EU CSRD remains the most comprehensive global standard. Following the Sustainability Omnibus update in early 2026, the EU raised its reporting thresholds to capture only the largest firms, specifically those with over 1,000 employees and €450 million in net turnover." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

*Analytical inference:* The combination of a stalled federal rule and active state-level and EU mandates creates an asymmetric compliance burden: clients of the workspace operating in California or EU jurisdictions face real and active Scope 3 disclosure obligations, even as US federal pressure has temporarily receded. This asymmetry is the commercial pressure point for freight forwarders — the clients who need data are still present, even if the federal enforcer has stepped back.

**Axis 3 — AI as an operational decarbonisation tool.** A peer-reviewed study published in MDPI Sustainability (October 2025) examines AI's role in "proactive supply chain emission avoidance" across logistics, agri-food, and textile sectors. The journal is indexed as an open-access peer-reviewed publication. *Source: MDPI Sustainability, "AI-Driven Supply Chain Decarbonization: Strategies for Sustainable Carbon Reduction," MDPI, October 2025. https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/21/9642

*Specific content of the MDPI study's findings not fully extractable from the available source block — the source page rendered with JavaScript-dependent content partially unavailable. The journal title and subject matter are confirmed from the source URL and page metadata; specific quantitative findings from this study are treated as a research gap pending full-text retrieval.*

---

# Section 2: Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Named parties and stated interests, sourced:**

**Trellis Group (formerly GreenBiz)** operates as the primary editorial and convening body for corporate sustainability strategy across supply chains. Its supply chain topic hub aggregates coverage from corporate strategy, decarbonisation, circular economy, and technology angles. *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, April 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/

**The Greenhouse Gas Protocol** is actively consulting on an expansion of emissions accounting methodology. Trellis coverage specifically notes "new guidelines, released this week, for how companies can claim benefits for investments in supply-chain decarbonization." *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, April 14, 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/

*Analytical inference:* The GHG Protocol's proposed expansion is being driven by corporate demand for a credible method to claim credit for supply chain investment — suggesting that large cargo-principal clients of freight forwarders are seeking accounting frameworks that reward supplier-level decarbonisation, which would include freight decarbonisation.

**California regulators (CARB / California legislature)** are advancing SB 253 and SB 261 as state-level replacements for the stalled federal SEC rule. SB 261 (climate-related financial risk) is noted as "currently under a temporary judicial stay from the Ninth Circuit, though companies are encouraged to remain 'publish-ready' as a ruling could resume enforcement at any time." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**New York, New Jersey, Colorado, and Illinois** are identified as states "attempting to introduce laws requiring large businesses to report GHG emissions." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**The EU Commission** has proceeded with CSRD implementation, with the Sustainability Omnibus update in early 2026 raising thresholds but preserving the double materiality obligation. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**BCG** has published a 2025 report framing supply chain decarbonisation as a liability-to-advantage transition. Specific content from BCG's report was not extractable from the available source block — the BCG page rendered as a navigation/services index without article body content. BCG's general positioning on supply chain decarbonisation is confirmed by the source's title and URL; specific quantitative claims from this report are treated as a research gap pending full-text retrieval. *Source: BCG, "Liability to Advantage: Decarbonizing the Supply Chain," BCG, 2025. https://www.bcg.com/publications/2025/liability-to-advantage-decarbonizing-supply-chain

---

# Section 3: Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**California SB 253 — conversion already occurred.** The August 10, 2026 Scope 1 and Scope 2 deadline for large enterprises doing business in California is a live, active obligation. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

*Analytical inference:* For the workspace, this means clients in scope for California SB 253 are already requesting or will shortly request emissions data from logistics providers, including freight forwarders, to populate their Scope 3 inventory ahead of the August 10, 2026 deadline. The conversion from signal to active commercial pressure for freight-forwarder data supply has already occurred for California-connected trade flows.

**Scope 3 reporting under California SB 253** is scheduled for 2027. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

*Analytical inference:* The 2027 Scope 3 deadline is the higher-stakes trigger for freight forwarders, as logistics emissions are typically classified as Scope 3 (Category 4: upstream transportation; Category 9: downstream transportation) in the GHG Protocol framework. A workspace that can provide auditable, methodology-consistent emissions data per shipment is positioned ahead of competitors who cannot.

**EU CSRD — double materiality obligation active for large firms.** "Double materiality" requires companies to report on both financial materiality (how climate change affects the company's value) and impact materiality (how the company's activities impact the environment). *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**GHG Protocol expansion — pre-conversion.** The expansion is under consultation as of April 2026. A stated schedule for finalisation is not available from the sourced content. *Analytical inference:* Based on GHG Protocol's historical consultation-to-publication cycles (typically 12–24 months after new guideline release), a finalised expanded standard could arrive 2027–2028, though this estimate is not sourced and should be confirmed when the Protocol publishes its consultation timeline.

**Ninth Circuit ruling on California SB 261** is pending. The timing of the ruling is not available from the sourced content. The workspace should monitor for any ruling that lifts the judicial stay.

---

# Section 4: Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**Filtered by air (primary), road (secondary), ocean (tertiary) — and by the workspace's role as freight forwarder:**

**Air-primary operations:**
*Operational implication:* Clients shipping time-critical, high-value cargo by air who are subject to California SB 253 or EU CSRD will require per-shipment or per-lane emissions data from the workspace. Air freight generates significantly higher emissions per tonne-kilometre than road or ocean; without a documented methodology and data supply capability, the workspace is exposed to being excluded from RFPs that include emissions data requirements.

*Analytical inference:* For the workspace, the air-freight emissions data gap is the most urgent, because air lanes are where per-shipment emissions intensity is highest, making the data both hardest to estimate without tooling and most material to a client's Scope 3 inventory.

**Road operations:**
*Operational implication:* Road drayage and secondary surface moves connected to air or port operations carry embedded emissions. For clients filing under California SB 253, the Scope 3 boundary likely captures road segments operated by or on behalf of the forwarder. The methodology used for road emissions calculation (distance-based vs. spend-based) will affect reported figures significantly.

**Ocean-tertiary operations:**
*Operational implication:* EU CSRD clients with ocean shipments face the most developed regulatory emissions reporting environment (FuelEU Maritime, EU ETS for shipping). For ocean segments, carrier-provided emissions data through tools such as the IMO's Data Collection System or shipper-oriented reporting platforms is more mature than for air. The workspace should assess whether its ocean carrier partners provide per-shipment CO2 data today.

**Cost of data infrastructure:**
*Specific cost figure for emissions data platform implementation not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-28.*

*Analytical inference:* The cost of building or procuring a shipment-level emissions data capability is an investment against the risk of client attrition or RFP exclusion. Whether this is a cost centre or a margin-protective investment depends on the share of the workspace's client base subject to active disclosure obligations — which is currently highest for California-connected and EU-connected clients.

---

# Section 5: Competitive Implications

*No specific named competitor positioning for freight forwarders on supply chain emissions data capability was available from the sourced content. The following is labeled inference only.*

*Analytical inference:* Large integrated logistics providers (operating across air, road, and ocean at global scale) have invested earlier and more heavily in per-shipment CO2 reporting tools. A mid-scale freight forwarder without a documented emissions methodology is at a structural disadvantage in RFPs from California-scope or EU-CSRD-scope clients who require Scope 3 data from their logistics providers.

*Analytical inference:* The GHG Protocol's proposed expansion of emissions accounting — specifically guidelines allowing companies to "claim benefits for investments in supply-chain decarbonization" — could create a differentiation mechanism: forwarders who can document decarbonisation investments (SAF uplift, lower-emission routing, modal shift) may generate a reportable credit for their clients, converting the forwarder's sustainability investment into a client-facing commercial asset.

*Industry interpretation:* EcoVadis frames the environment for 2026 as one where "Major investors and state-level authorities have moved forward with distinct mandates, making proactive reporting critical for maintaining market access and meeting stakeholder expectations." *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

---

# Section 6: Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say — sourced basis:**

- California SB 253 creates a firm August 10, 2026 deadline for Scope 1 and 2 reporting for large enterprises doing business in California, with Scope 3 following in 2027. *(Sourced fact — cite EcoVadis above.)*
- The EU CSRD threshold was raised in early 2026 to firms with over 1,000 employees and €450M turnover, but the double materiality obligation — covering both the company's exposure to climate risk and its own impact on the environment — remains in force for in-scope firms. *(Sourced fact — cite EcoVadis above.)*
- The US federal SEC rule is effectively suspended, but state-level and EU obligations remain active and create real reporting requirements for clients operating in those jurisdictions. *(Sourced fact — cite EcoVadis above.)*
- The GHG Protocol is actively consulting on new guidelines for how companies can claim benefits from supply chain decarbonisation investments — positioning supply chain sustainability spend as potentially reportable value, not just cost. *(Sourced fact — cite Trellis above.)*

**Questions to pose to clients:**
- Are you doing business in California, and do you meet the SB 253 threshold? If so, your Scope 3 reporting obligation begins in 2027 — what methodology are you using for logistics emissions today?
- Are you subject to EU CSRD? If so, what emissions data do you currently receive from your logistics providers, and is it auditable?
- Has your sustainability team assessed whether the GHG Protocol's proposed expansion affects how you account for supply chain decarbonisation investments?

**Pitfalls to avoid:**
- Do not claim the workspace's emissions data methodology meets any specific regulatory standard without legal and technical verification.
- Do not assert that the SEC rule is "dead" — it remains formally on the books; federal enforcement is suspended, not the rule itself. *Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/
- Do not cite BCG or MDPI study-specific quantitative findings in client conversations without first reading the full reports; the source blocks available for this brief did not render their article bodies.

---

# Section 7: What the Workspace Should Do Now

Listed by action urgency, filtered by transport mode and cargo vertical:

**1. Map client exposure to California SB 253 and EU CSRD — now, before August 2026.**
*Action:* For each active client account, identify whether the client is (a) doing business in California at a scale likely to meet SB 253 thresholds or (b) subject to EU CSRD. This mapping determines which clients will request Scope 3 data from the workspace in 2026–2027.
*Why now:* California SB 253 Scope 1 and 2 deadline is August 10, 2026. Scope 3 reporting follows in 2027. Clients who have not yet established their logistics emissions methodology are behind, and the workspace can be a proactive data partner rather than a reactive supplier.

**2. Assess per-shipment emissions data capability — air lanes first.**
*Action:* Determine whether the workspace can currently produce per-shipment CO2e figures for air shipments using a documented, reproducible methodology (e.g., GLEC Framework or ISO 14083-consistent approach). If not, identify the gap and the fastest path to close it.
*Why air first:* Air freight emissions per tonne-kilometre are the highest of the three modes the workspace operates, making air-lane emissions the most material line item in a client's Scope 3 logistics category and the most likely subject of client audit scrutiny.

**3. Monitor GHG Protocol consultation on emissions accounting expansion.**
*Action:* Subscribe to GHG Protocol updates and assign a team member to track the consultation on new supply chain decarbonisation accounting guidelines (noted as released April 2026). Set a re-check window: **Q3 2026**, when consultation feedback periods typically close and a draft standard may be published.
*Why:* If the proposed expansion creates a mechanism for clients to claim credit for forwarder-level decarbonisation investments, the workspace's SAF purchases, fleet electrification spend, or modal shift data become client-reportable assets — a commercial differentiator.

**4. Monitor Ninth Circuit ruling on California SB 261.**
*Action:* The temporary judicial stay on SB 261 (climate-related financial risk disclosure) could be lifted at any time. Set a monitoring flag. Re-check: **quarterly through end 2026**.
*Source: EcoVadis, "SEC Climate Disclosure Rule: Current Status and What to Know in 2026," EcoVadis, May 5, 2026. https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/

**5. Track GreenBiz/Trellis Impact 26 — June 23–25, San Francisco.**
*Action:* Trellis Impact 26 is a confirmed event with sourced dates. *Source: Trellis Group Supply Chain Topic Hub, Trellis Group, 2026. https://trellis.net/topic/supply-chain/ This is the primary convening moment for corporate sustainability and supply chain decarbonisation decision-makers in 2026. Client contacts who attend this event will return with updated expectations. Brief commercial and operations leads before the event; debrief clients who attend.

**6. Retrieve full text of BCG and MDPI reports before citing them.**
*Action:* The BCG "Liability to Advantage" report (2025) and the MDPI AI-driven supply chain decarbonisation study (October 2025) are cited in industry conversations but their full content was not available in the source blocks for this brief. Retrieve both before using them in client presentations or RFP responses. Adding unread research to client-facing materials is a credibility risk.

---
